title: Stakeholder Models
summary: Three ways to classify stakeholders by influence - the Power/Interest grid, the Salience model, and the Stakeholder Cube - plus the engagement-assessment matrix and how to choose between them, framed by the PMBOK 8 Stakeholders performance domain and principle.
intro: Classify and engage stakeholders - salience, power/interest and the cube.

sections.heading: Why classify stakeholders
sections.body: You can't engage everyone the same way, so stakeholder analysis sorts people by how much they can affect the project and how affected they are by it. In PMBOK 8 terms this serves the Stakeholders performance domain (productive relationships and agreement on value) and the Stakeholders principle (engage stakeholders effectively to support value and satisfaction). The output drives your engagement plan: who to manage closely, who to keep satisfied, who to keep informed, and who to simply monitor.

blocks.title: Living, not one-and-done
blocks.text: All three are classification tools used during stakeholder identification and analysis. None is 'better' - they trade simplicity for nuance. People move between categories as the project, their interest, and their urgency change, so re-map at major milestones and tailor engagement to the context.

sections.heading: Power/Interest grid
sections.body: The most common model. It plots each stakeholder on two axes - power (their ability to influence the outcome) and interest (how much they care about it) - creating four quadrants, each with a standard engagement strategy. Close relatives swap one axis: the Power/Influence grid uses active involvement instead of interest, and the Influence/Impact grid uses ability to change the plan.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='44' y1='16' x2='44' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='44' y1='172' x2='306' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='175' y1='16' x2='175' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='44' y1='94' x2='306' y2='94' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><text x='109' y='58' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Keep Satisfied</text><text x='240' y='58' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Manage Closely</text><text x='109' y='136' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Monitor</text><text x='240' y='136' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Keep Informed</text><text x='26' y='94' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 26 94)'>Power</text><text x='175' y='195' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Interest</text></svg>
figure.caption: Power up the side, interest across the bottom; each quadrant has its own strategy.

lines: Involve them and meet their expectations.
Enough attention that they don't become blockers.

blocks.title: Memory cue
blocks.text: Memorise the quadrants by power first: high power = Manage Closely (also interested) or Keep Satisfied (not). Low power = Keep Informed (interested) or Monitor (not).

sections.heading: Salience model
sections.body: The salience model adds a third dimension. It scores each stakeholder on three attributes and uses the overlaps of a Venn diagram to set priority - the more attributes someone holds, the more 'salient' (prominent) they are. Its benefit is a sharper read on whose needs to prioritise, so you spend communication effort where it counts; its limitation is that scoring three attributes is more involved than a simple grid, and the labels can feel judgemental.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 227 238' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='68' cy='86.5' r='56' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='148' cy='86.5' r='56' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='108' cy='148.5' r='56' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='68' y='22.5' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>Power</text><text x='148' y='22.5' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>Legitimacy</text><text x='108' y='222.5' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>Urgency</text><text x='32' y='92.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dormant</text><text x='178' y='92.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Discretionary</text><text x='108' y='186.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Demanding</text><rect x='89' y='55.5' width='38' height='10' fill='%BG%'/><text x='108' y='63.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dominant</text><rect x='48' y='125.5' width='44' height='10' fill='%BG%'/><text x='70' y='133.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dangerous</text><rect x='124' y='125.5' width='44' height='10' fill='%BG%'/><text x='146' y='133.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dependent</text><rect x='81' y='99.5' width='54' height='12' fill='%BG%'/><text x='108' y='108.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Definitive</text></svg>
figure.caption: Three attributes overlap; a stakeholder with all three (Definitive) is highest priority.

rows.value: Can they influence the outcome or withhold resources?
Is their involvement proper and authorised (a sponsor, owner, regulator)?
Is their claim time-critical (deadline-driven), or does only the eventual outcome matter regardless of timing?

groups.label: Dormant (power only)
lines: e.g. a senior manager in another division.

groups.label: Discretionary (legitimacy only)
lines: e.g. an NGO, or a team member giving feedback.

groups.label: Demanding (urgency only)
lines: e.g. the CEO's assistant chasing information.

groups.label: Dominant (power + legitimacy)
lines: e.g. your boss, or a government clearance office.

groups.label: Dependent (legitimacy + urgency)
lines: No power, so they need a champion or coalition.
e.g. local residents affected by construction.

groups.label: Dangerous (power + urgency)
lines: No legitimacy - coercive, may bypass process.
e.g. a group threatening disruptive action.

groups.label: Definitive
lines: The centre of the diagram, your highest priority.
e.g. your executive sponsor or the key customer.

rows.value: Dangerous - keep satisfied to contain the threat.
Outside all three circles - no power, legitimacy or urgency, so no active engagement.

blocks.title: Urgency is volatile
blocks.text: Urgency spikes with deadlines and crises. A Discretionary or Dependent stakeholder can jump to Definitive overnight, so re-score regularly.

sections.heading: Stakeholder Cube
sections.body: The cube takes the 2D Power/Interest grid and extrudes it into three dimensions, adding attitude (supportive 'backer' vs. opposed 'blocker'). Combining power (high/low), interest (active/passive), and attitude (backer/blocker) gives eight stakeholder 'characters', each a corner of the cube. Its value is that it makes attitude explicit - two stakeholders with identical power and interest may need opposite handling depending on whether they back or block you.

figure.caption: Power x interest x attitude - eight corners, from Saviour (high power, active, backer) to Irritant.

groups.label: Saboteur
lines: The biggest threat; manage and contain.

groups.label: Sleeping giant
lines: Wake them up - a powerful supporter not yet engaged.

groups.label: Time bomb
lines: Dormant opposition that could detonate; watch closely.

groups.label: Trip wire
lines: Minor but vocal opposition; can trip you up.

blocks.title: What to remember
blocks.text: The cube is the most detailed but least commonly tested. Know that it adds attitude (backer vs. blocker) to power and interest, and that a high-power blocker (Saboteur / Time bomb) is the corner to fear.

sections.heading: Engagement-assessment matrix
sections.body: Classification tells you who matters; the stakeholder engagement-assessment matrix tracks how engaged each one actually is versus how engaged they need to be. Mark Current (C) and Desired (D) for each stakeholder across five levels - a gap between C and D is where the engagement plan focuses effort. This is the working artifact of the Stakeholders performance domain.

rows.value: Does not know about the project or its impact.
Aware but opposed to the work or its outcomes.
Aware but neither supportive nor resistant.
Aware and supportive of the work and outcomes.
Aware and actively engaged in ensuring success.

blocks.title: Close the gap
lines: Mark C = current and D = desired for each stakeholder.
Where C and D differ, plan engagement actions to move them.
The matrix drives the plan; classification models feed it.

sections.heading: Choosing a model
sections.body: All three are valid PMI classification models - pick the one that fits the situation, then tailor engagement to context. Whatever you use, classification feeds the stakeholder register and the engagement plan, and it is a living judgement, not a one-time label.

rows.value: Fast, intuitive; good for most projects and for communicating with leadership.
When timing and legitimacy matter - regulators, crisis response, or stakeholders whose urgency swings.
When attitude is decisive and you need to separate supporters from opponents who otherwise look identical.

blocks.title: Exam clue
lines: Current vs desired engagement = the engagement-assessment matrix.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
